data.title: Hims & Hers Health: Bumpy Ride Will End
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) has hit a bumpy ride over the last year, with the stock stuck in a range from $40 to $70. The online health and wellness platform has faced a lot of questions regarding compounding GLP-1s, with the latest news being a letter from the FDA. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the stock, as the company moves forward with growth plans while the GLP-1 business was never the long-term focus.

FDA Questions

Hims dipped 6% on the U.S. FDA sending a letter to the company questioning the marketing of their GLP-1 compounds. The FDA had previously questioned the Super Bowl ad from Hims & Hers Health with Marty Makary, Commissioner of the U.S. FDA, pointing out the ad failed to mention side effects of compounded GLP-1 drugs.

The stock rebounded from larger losses as the CEO went on Twitter to claim the FDA sent similar letters to ~100 companies across the industry. Most importantly, big Pharma got those letters as well, so this isn't anything specific where Hims & Hers Health is doing something egregious with compounded GLP-1s.

As usual, the stock market sells first and asks questions later. Hims & Hers is down on fears the FDA is signaling out the online health platform when in reality the scrutiny of health regulators is likely to reduce a lot of the competition in the online health space.

On the Q2 earnings report, Hims again pointed out the weight-loss drug segment was targeted at a $725 million business in 2025, with GLP-1s accounting for a portion of that business. The company has forecast 2025 revenue of $2.35 billion, so the weight-loss segment is just 31% of total revenues.

The investment story was never based solely on compounded GLP-1 drugs and the partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO) never really added up, with the platform not really built for re-selling branded drugs. Hims did report Q2 revenue of $545 million, which dipped sequentially by $41 million due to the hiccup of transitioning patients off the commercially available GLP-1s, but the company is already back to reporting sequential growth with the Q3'25 target of at least $580 million.

Focus on Future

The market cap has fallen to an $11.5 billion market cap, with the stock only trading at 4x 2026 sales targets of just $2.8 billion. Hims will likely see solid gains on these targets once all of the noise around compounded GLP-1s leaves the story.

Hims just launched new offerings for low testosterone and is moving full-speed forward with at-home tests after acquiring a blood testing lab. The company sees a market opportunity in hormonal health of 50 million patients in the U.S. underrecognized by traditional healthcare.

The company just closed the Zava acquisition and targets a $50 million revenue potential for 2025. On the Q2'25 earnings call, the CEO outlined a large opportunity in the international markets despite investor concerns on government healthcare outside the U.S.:

Ultimately, we believe the international market is a focused effort, right? It's not a spray and pray model. We don't believe that there are dozens of markets that are required for a substantial revenue footprint. I think long term, we believe there's a multibillion-dollar revenue opportunity in just a handful of key markets. And I think you'll see us in the next 1 to 3 years going after those focused markets.

Hims remains a huge play on personalized services, but what the market has probably missed is that the online platform moved beyond solving just one medical condition. At the end of Q2, the company has nearly 500K subscribers utilizing personalized treatment plans to target multiple conditions, up nearly 170% YoY.

The online health and wellness platform continues to innovate by looking for solutions to problems not solved by the current medical system and has moved far beyond simple ED pills. The long-term goal is to turn Hims & Hers Health in a membership platform to cover the majority of medical conditions impacting patients, with the business transformed to the goal of preventing medical issues versus treating them.

The company has 2030 financial targets of at least $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA, but these are just stepping stones on the path to 10+ million subscribers. Hims obtains $74 per month from existing subscribers, and 10 million subs at this monthly revenue would generate $8.9 billion in annual revenues, far above current estimates.

The stock has been stuck in a range from $40 to $70 over most of the year. As the company returns to sequential sales growth and moves beyond this volatile GLP-1 period, Hims should trade higher.

Naturally, the risk is to run into problems with compounded GLP-1 sales. In addition, the online health and wellness platform has substantial competition that could slow down the growth of the business.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Hims trades at a cheap valuation despite explosive growth and huge catalysts for the next decade. The stock is down on the drama with GLP-1s, but the business is moving far beyond a focus on weight-loss drugs, making the recent weakness a buying opportunity.

data.title: Fed Pivots: What The Rate Cut Means For Bonds, Gold, And AI Stocks
data.description:
The Federal Reserve delivered the expected 25-basis-point rate cut. There is a clear shift in tone and direction. Especially in the Q&A, it sounded like a pivotal meeting. Powell appeared concerned about the job market and not very worried about inflation anymore.

Why isn't he worried about inflation when it is at 3% and has been there for a long time now? One answer is because the Fed views tariffs as a temporary influence that can’t be combatted by hiking rates. Another likely reason (which they aren't communicating in so many words) is because it sees the economy cooling off. I'll go over a few key quotes from the Fed's Q&A session. All emphasis has been put there by me.

A journalist asked him if the balance of risks was no longer pointing towards restrictive policy (if they aren't, they are pointing towards easing policy)

MR. POWELL: So I don’t – I don’t think we can say – we can say that. What we can say is this, that over the course of this year we’ve kept our policy at a restrictive level – and people have different views, but a clearly restrictive level, I would say. So – and we were able to do that over the course of this year because the labor market was in very solid condition with strong job creation and all those things.

I think if you go back to April and now look at the revised job creation numbers for May, June, July, and August, you can kind of – I can no longer say that. So what that means is that the risks, which – the risks were clearly tilted toward inflation. I would say they’re moving toward equality. Maybe they’re not quite at equality. We don’t need to know that. But we got to – we do know that they’ve moved meaningfully toward greater equality, the risks between the two goals, and that suggests that we should be moving in the direction of neutral. And that’s what we did today.

This is early in the Q&A when he's still trying to sound hawkish. But he gets asked about the jobs market repeatedly:

MR. POWELL: So it’s an interesting labor market. And obviously we think it’s appropriate that we reduce our rates, so that we become more neutral, which will be – which presumably will be better for the labor market. You see people who are sort of more at the margins – so kids coming out of college and younger people, minorities – are having a hard time finding jobs. The overall job finding rate is very, very low. However, the layoff rate is also very low. So you’ve got a low – a low firing, low hiring environment.

And the concern is that if you start to see layoffs, the people who are laid off won’t – there won’t be a lot of hiring going on. So that could very quickly flow into higher unemployment. In a healthier economy, healthier labor market, there would be jobs for those people. But now the hiring rate is very, very low. So that’s been a growing concern over the last few months. And it’s one of the reasons why we think it’s appropriate that we begin to shift our policy focus toward a more balanced one.

Powell also believes more restrictive immigration policies have resulted in lower labor supply. This seems to be backed by data indicating a rare decline in the U.S. foreign-born population. To an extent this balanced out a fall in labor demand.

The number of immigrants is obviously much lower than the number of employed people. If the demand for workers is declining, this can be counteracted by decreasing the inflow through immigration only to a limited extent. If demand for workers continues to decline, it becomes more obvious as this buffering force is gone.

Years prior, Powell had been very consistent in pointing out the danger of sticky inflation and reiterating the Fed wants to push it down. Even at times accepting that it would limit the buoyancy of the job market. Here’s how he explains why he’s switching gears:

Right now, the situation we’re in is that we see inflation – we continue to expect it to move up. Maybe not as high as we would have expected it to move up a few months ago. The pass through into – of the tariffs into inflation has been slower and smaller. The labor market has softened. So the case for there being a persistent inflation outbreak is less. And so that’s why we think it’s time for us really to acknowledge that the risks to the other mandate have grown, and that we should move in the direction of neutral. So what will we do? We’ll do what we need to do. But we have two mandates, and we try to balance them.

For a long time, our framework says that when our two goals are in tension – this is quite an unusual situation – how do we decide what to do? Because our tools can’t do two things at once. What we do is we ask, which is farther from – how far is each from the goal and how long is it expected to get to the goal? So and then that’s – we think about those things, and we see – as I mentioned, we have been – our policy had been really skewed toward inflation for a long time, really. Now we see that there’s downside risk clearly in the labor market. And so we’re moving in a direction of more neutral policy.

He even sounded somewhat defensive, justifying that it is only a 25bp cut by pointing out they could do more in the future:

So we put all this together and we see that that the labor market is softening. And we don’t need it to soften any more, don’t want it to. So we use our tools. And, you know, it starts with a 25 basis point rate cut, but we – you know, the market’s also pricing in a rate path. I’m not blessing what the market’s doing at all. I’m just saying it’s not just one action.

This went from "moving towards a more neutral stance" because the balance is tilting away from having to guard against inflation to "doing more in the future".

For the first time in a very long time, I think the 10-year treasury could be very attractive. The yield is above 4%. Employment data tends to trend. The 10-year doesn’t work when inflation surges upwards. However, it may not be bothered by tariff (temporary) related inflation impact. If the labor market continues to cool, this will impact growth. Which becomes a strong disinflationary force. The Fed appears ready and willing to cut into a 3%+ inflation scenario (depending on the underlying cause), and the administration is eager to appoint a dovish new chair in 2026. If the labor market/economy stabilizes, I’d be very worried about the push for dovish monetary policy. An example of an ETF benefitting from that trend is the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF)

Things continue to look great for gold. The price chart above shows the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) year-to-date result of 37.99%. A slowdown or recession isn't necessarily bad for gold. Even the truly bad scenario of stagflation isn’t bad for gold. It tends to be viewed as a safe haven asset. If bonds start doing really well (with clear disinflation), it could draw money away. The scenario where gold would be most likely to struggle—continued rate hikes by the Fed and the administration pushing for a strong dollar—seems quite far-fetched at this point.

Equities, exemplified by the S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) and the dollar (USD), are starting to look like some of the more risky places. Counterintuitively, I expect some of the "hyped" AI hardware companies to perform relatively well. Think NVIDIA (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductors (TSM), etc.

An important way to get ahead in the race between models is by throwing increasingly vast amounts of compute towards the problem. That will continue to be a reliable way forward. Hopefully it gets more and more supported by more cost-efficient innovation, but it almost guarantees progress.

There’s a large advantage to having a top-tier model in terms of attracting customers, attracting funding, cost of capital, and getting broad data on how customers are interacting with these models. True, a recession could cut some of the contenders off from capital. At some point several players are going to drop out of the top-tier race anyway. However, in the near term I’d expect spending to remain very high. It is either spend or give up.

Some of the companies doing this spending are more vulnerable than they would otherwise be during a slowdown: Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META), among others. It's not that I would short them, but it is something to be aware of.

data.title: We Still Like Darden
data.description:
After a nice profitable trade on Darden Restaurants, Inc. (NYSE:DRI), we have been advocating maintaining a house position consisting of a portion of the profit for long-term returns. At BAD BEAT Investing, we love this approach to generational wealth creation. The beauty of holding a portfolio of hundreds of house positions in profitable trades is that it allows you to receive all future dividends, gains, spinoffs, etc. long-term, while allocating principal to other short-term trading opportunities. Rinse and repeat. Darden has been doing well since the trade, though it is pulling back some today following the just reported fiscal Q1 and the outlook.

Darden fiscal Q1 revenue

This was a missed quarter overall, with a very slight miss on revenues and a $0.04 miss on the EPS line. Let's start with sales. In the competitive restaurant sector, comparable sales are a key measure we continue to monitor for restaurants. In the just-reported fiscal Q1 2025, Darden did see growth in sales versus the prior year. Revenue was up 8.7% to $3.0 billion this quarter. As mentioned, it was a very slight miss versus consensus estimates by $40 million.

Darden fiscal Q1 comparable sales

How about the critical comparable sales figure? Well, they were up overall, with some mixed performance in fine dining. Comparable sales for the entire company were up 4.7%. This is big positive. When we dig into the comparable sales by each operating restaurant segment, we see that DRI's LongHorn Steakhouse continues to be the big winner, while Olive Garden really surged. Fine dining was the only main segment with comp declines, and so-called "other business" was up too.

Under the LongHorn Steakhouse banner, comparable sales were up a strong 5.5%. The Olive Garden banner led the way with comps rising 5.9%. The fine dining part of the business was once again a bit weak with comparable sales falling 0.2%. It would be great if they would break this out a bit more so we knew where there was strength and weakness within the line. The so-called “other business” rose 3.3%.

Rick Cardenas, Darden President & CEO, stated in the earnings release:

We had a strong start to the fiscal year with same-restaurant sales and earnings growth that exceeded our expectations. The strength of our results is a testament to the power of our strategy. Across our portfolio, our restaurant teams remained focused on being brilliant with the basics and, at the Darden level, we continued to leverage our four competitive advantages to position our brands for long-term success. Our winning strategy is enabling us to grow sales and market share, while making meaningful investments in our business and returning capital to our shareholders

While it was mixed it was a decent quarter overall, considering the economic mix and what we are seeing in jobs data. While it was mixed on some lines, the market is rewarding the stock. What about profit? There have been some margin concerns. Segment profit followed the sales trends. Olive Garden saw a profit of $267.6 million vs. $250.1 million last year. The LongHorn Steakhouse banner had a segment profit of $134.9 million, rising from $128.4 million a year ago. Fine dining profit was about flat at $38.7 million, versus $38.9 million a year ago, while the other business banners saw a nice 30% increase in profit, rising to $109.3 million. Factoring in expenses, Darden reported adjusted EPS of $1.97, which missed estimates by $0.04.

We also like the share repurchases and dividends. With this morning's dividend announcement of $1.50 per share for the quarter, the stock still provides a 3% yield with today's pull back. That is a 10 cent per quarter boost from the old dividend of $1.40 quarterly. We do expect ongoing dividend increases in the future. Darden bought back $183 million worth of shares in the quarter. At this level, we think the valuation is balanced for the growth here, in our opinion, justifying a comfortable hold rating.

The guidance was positive as well overall. It upped sales growth to 7.5% to 8.5% for the year, higher on the low end. Same store sales should grow 3% at the midpoint while new restaurant openings were upped to 65 openings from 60-65 previously. Further EPS should come in at $10.60 at the midpoint. At $190 per share this is pretty fair value for the moderate growth.

Final thoughts

We like Darden Restaurants, Inc.'s shareholder-friendly policies here with the dividend and buybacks, but view shares as pretty fairly valued. We continue to like holding a house position and collecting long-term returns and cash flow from the growing dividends.

data.title: TBIL: The Fed Has Cut, What Is Next (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The US Treasury 3-Month Bill ETF (NASDAQ:TBIL) is a $6 billion fund and a reference point in the short term treasury fund cohort. The instrument is one of the most utilized cash parking vehicles, and we have covered this ETF before with a 'Buy' rating.

With the much anticipated Fed meeting from September 17, 2025, now behind us and a 25 bps cut, we are going to revisit TBIL and outline for investors what the recent cut means.

Buying T-Bills via an ETF

As a reminder, TBIL strives to provide exposure to the current 3-month Treasury Bill and thus represents a play on the front end of the yield curve. Its composition is reflective of that:

Please note the ETF holds three issuances, with the maximum maturity being December 2025.

Retail investors have the ability to buy T-Bills outright, but they have to go through the operational process of rolling them upon expiration. TBIL does that for you automatically, although there is a slight tax tweak one has to be aware of. This writer learned it the hard way when doing taxes - while outright T-Bills are reported automatically by brokerage companies as having exempt interest from state and local taxes, ETFs like TBIL need to have a manual calculation that needs inputting into tax software. Outside this slight reporting tweak, the fund represents a similar exposure to just rolling 3-month T-Bills.

If we take a look at historic correlations, we can see the one-for-one impact when Fed Funds are lowered:

The blue line in the chart above represents the Federal Funds Effective Rate, while the green line is the 3-month constant maturity T-Bills. Note the almost perfect correlation, with very short periods of very slight yield discrepancies. In essence this graph tells us that as the Fed cuts, 3-month T-Bills will follow Fed Funds lower.

The Fed has cut; what is next?

The Fed disappointed some market participants by having only a 25 bps cut, but two more cuts are priced in for the rest of 2025:

WASHINGTON – The Federal Reserve on Wednesday approved a widely anticipated rate cut and signaled that two more are on the way before the end of the year as concerns intensified over the U.S. labor market even as inflation is still in the air.

In an 11-to-1 vote signaling less dissent than Wall Street had anticipated, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark overnight lending rate by a quarter percentage point. The decision puts the overnight funds rate in a range between 4.00%-4.25%.

Chairman Powell mentioned that the decisions in the past regarding 50 bps cuts or increases were driven by the narrative of the Fed being behind what they thought would be an appropriate monetary stance. The current macro framework was described as being balanced, with less labor available balancing out less job availabilities:

“The marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers is unusual in this less dynamic and somewhat softer labor market,” he said. “The downside risks to employment appear to have risen.”

The topic of inflation was also discussed, with the Fed base-case including a one-time price increase from tariffs, rather than continuous inflation causing price moves.

While we can discuss and debate where monetary policy should be, one thing is for certain, and that is what the market is now pricing in:

The above graph plots the futures market for 1-month SOFR and the Fed Dot Plot. While the 'Dot Plot' is subject to debate, change, and political nominations, the SOFR futures market is not. The market is showing us deep cuts to come in the next year, with 1-month SOFR at 3% come December 2026.

We stated that the Fed Dot Plot is subject to various political nominations because we saw an interesting development in the latest Fed release. While most of the projections were quite similar, there was a Fed member who plotted an astounding 125 bps of cuts for the rest of 2025:

Only one official called for no cuts, while another member of the FOMC thought the central bank should cut rates by an additional 1.25% percentage point by the end of 2025. Because the dot plot is anonymous we don’t know for sure which member of the FOMC made each of these predictions. But given calls by the Administration for a significant cut in interest rates, it seems likely it was made by one of the President’s recent appointments.

The takeaway here is that the 'Dot Plot' projections are fickle, and investors are better served by looking at what the futures market is telling us.

Impact to TBIL

TBIL will see its 30-day SEC yield decrease as per the latest cut and will continue to embed almost one-for-one any future rate decreases. If indeed Fed Funds are at 3% by the end of 2026, do expect TBIL to yield something very similar at that point in time.

TBIL is purely an automated way to get exposure to short-dated T-Bills and the front of the yield curve and thus represents an almost direct take on Fed Funds. Should we be happy that TBIL will yield less? No. Is it in-line with what the fund is supposed to do? Absolutely.

Investors looking for protection from decreasing Fed Funds should look for longer-duration names, and we have covered such funds in the past:

  • SHY: Smart Duration Play, 4.9% Yield
  • NEAR: Still A Buy After The Rise In Rates

While there are other short-term funds that offer higher yields, they do come with either asset default risk or equity risk. Investors looking for clean treasury exposure should look only at higher-duration names.

Conclusion

TBIL is a cash parking vehicle composed of T-Bills. With a negligible duration and an almost perfect correlation to Fed Funds, the ETF represents a take on the front end of the yield curve. With the most recent Fed cut, we are going to see a lowering in the dividend here. Expect this dynamic to continue in the future as well - if we do get to 3% Fed Funds by the end of 2026, as the SOFR futures market is telling us, do expect the TBIL 30-day dividend yield to move down to that level as well. Investors looking for higher yields and protection from cuts should look at SHY and NEAR.
